program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Reading the minutes of a board meeting and relaying what was said -- reported speech (backshift, say vs tell, reported questions and commands) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- The Meeting, Relayed -- Reporting What Was Said
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 10 -- The Meeting, Relayed

listenings.text: Hi Izabel, this is Grace, the board secretary, with a recap of Thursday's meeting before I circulate the full minutes. The chair opened by saying that the year had been strong, and he explained that the sensor business had grown faster than anyone had expected. He added that the North American launch was now the top priority. The CFO then briefed the board on the numbers. She reported that the margin had improved, but she warned that overhead would rise if we opened a second plant too soon. On the client, you told the board that they had asked whether AVRT could shorten its lead time, and whether we would commit to a firm delivery date. The board agreed to clarify those two points before signing. In the end, everyone reached a consensus: they would endorse the launch, but defer the second plant for six months. I have two action items for you: the operations lead was asked to review the delivery schedule, and finance was asked to circulate an updated forecast. The key takeaway, as you put it, was that the board backs the plan but wants the numbers confirmed first. I will send the minutes this afternoon.

Izabel, it is Oscar, your advisor, with a quick debrief after the client call. Let me relay what they actually said, because it matters. Their head of procurement told me that the technical team had loved the sensor, and she admitted that your quality was ahead of every rival she had tested. Then she asked whether we could move the delivery date forward by three weeks, and whether the price would hold if the volume doubled. I explained that you would not cut the price, but that you would protect the lead time. She said that was fair. One warning: their CEO added that a competitor had approached them last month, so I would not defer our answer for long. My advice, and I put this in the debrief note I will circulate: brief your operations lead today, clarify the delivery date by Friday, and endorse the deal before the competitor does. The takeaway is simple. They want us, but they want speed. Call me after you have read the minutes.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the official written record of what was said in a meeting
the list of topics a meeting will discuss
to give someone the key information they need
a short summary of the main points
to make something clearer or remove confusion
to give official or public support to something
to postpone a decision to a later time

paras: On Thursday, AVRT's board met to review the year. As CEO, Izabel set the agenda, chaired the meeting, and later circulated the minutes to the whole leadership team. The chair opened by thanking everyone. He said that the results had been strong, and he explained that the sensor business had grown faster than the board had expected. He added that the North American launch was now the top priority for the following year.

The CFO then briefed the board and gave a short recap of the numbers. She reported that the margin had improved, but she warned that overhead would rise if the company opened a second plant too soon. A client who had joined by video was next on the agenda. Izabel told the board that the client had asked whether AVRT could shorten its lead time, and whether the team would commit to a firm delivery date. The board agreed to clarify those two points before signing anything.

Not everything was decided. One director asked if the company should defer the second plant until the volume was certain. After some discussion, the board reached a consensus: they would endorse the launch, but defer the new plant for six months. Before closing, Izabel assigned two action items. She told the operations lead to review the delivery schedule, and she asked the finance team to circulate an updated forecast. She also promised to debrief the sales team the next morning. The key takeaway, she wrote in the minutes, was simple: the board backed the plan, but wanted the numbers confirmed first.
reading.source: Adapted from internal board minutes, AVRT Leadership Review

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The board rejected the North American launch and cancelled the plan.
Izabel relays what was said at the board meeting: the plan is endorsed, but the new plant is deferred and the numbers must be confirmed.
The meeting ended with no decisions and no agreement at all.

items: The chair said that the sensor business had grown faster than expected.
'He explained that the sensor business had grown faster than the board had expected.'
The CFO said that overhead would fall if a second plant opened.
'she warned that overhead would rise if the company opened a second plant too soon.'
The board decided to endorse the launch but defer the new plant.
'they would endorse the launch, but defer the new plant for six months.'
Izabel told the operations lead to circulate the forecast.
'She told the operations lead to review the delivery schedule, and she asked the finance team to circulate an updated forecast.'

shorten its lead time. Izabel told the operations lead
the schedule, and the board reached a

gapfill.text: Reported speech: when we relay what someone said, the verb usually shifts back one tense (backshift). Present -> past ('is' -> 'was', 'has grown' -> 'had grown'); will -> would; can -> could. Use 'tell + someone' but 'say' with no person ('She told the board...', 'He said that...'). For reported questions, use 'ask if / whether' and keep normal word order: 'She asked whether we could deliver' (NOT 'asked can we deliver').

bank.label: Useful language for relaying a meeting
items: The chair said that the results had been strong.
She reported that the margin had improved.
He explained that the launch was now the priority.
The client asked whether we could shorten the lead time.
The board agreed to clarify the delivery date.
Izabel told the operations lead to review the schedule.

lf.title: Reported speech: which form?
items: The chair said that the results
The CFO reported that the margin

lf.text: Backshift: present -> past, will -> would, can -> could, is/are -> was/were, has/have -> had. Use 'told + someone' (told the board) but 'said' without a person (said that...). Reported questions use 'if / whether' with normal word order and no 'do' support. Reported commands use 'told + someone + to + base verb': She told us to review the budget.

items: The chair said, 'The results are strong.' Relay it in reported speech, starting 'The chair said that...'.
A client asked, 'Can you shorten the lead time?' Relay it as a reported question with 'whether'.
You said to the operations lead, 'Review the delivery schedule.' Relay it as a reported command with 'told'.
Relay one real thing a colleague or client told you this week, using said / told / explained and correct backshift.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = had been, 2 = had improved, 3 = would, 4 = could, 5 = to review, 6 = consensus.
Scenario 1: 'The chair said that the results were strong.'
Scenario 2: 'The client asked whether we could shorten the lead time.' (no inversion, no 'do')
Scenario 3: 'I told the operations lead to review the delivery schedule.'
answerkey.note: Reported speech = backshift one tense (present -> past, will -> would, can -> could). 'Tell + someone', 'say' with no person. Reported questions: ask if/whether, normal word order. Reported commands: told + someone + to + base.
